one_line_takeaway: A pattern catalog arguing that prompt-injection resistance in LLM agents should come from system-level isolation that constrains post-untrusted-data actions, trading agent generality for security, illustrated across ten application case studies rather than measured on benchmarks.
key_finding: Meaningful, near-guaranteed resistance to prompt injection is achievable not by hardening the model but by architecturally constraining what an agent can do after touching untrusted data, at a deliberate cost to generality. The patterns form a spectrum: Action-Selector gives the strongest guarantee (the LLM never sees untrusted data directly, 'trivially immune') but sacrifices most fuzzy-reasoning utility; Plan-Then-Execute and Code-Then-Execute provide control-flow-integrity (the plan/program of actions cannot be altered by injected data) but still let untrusted data influence action PARAMETERS (e.g., an injection in calendar data can still alter an email body); Map-Reduce and Dual LLM isolate untrusted data into quarantined sub-agents whose outputs are constrained (e.g., forced to a boolean/number), limiting a malicious document's blast radius to itself; Context-Minimization strips the user prompt from context after it has been converted to a query, defeating user-side injection into post-processing. The paper is candid that these are trade-offs, not free lunches, and that residual risks remain (parameter tampering, quarantined-LLM output still being attacker-controlled sensitive data, decompositions that are impossible for some tasks).
core_contribution: A conceptual systematization of six named design patterns for building LLM agents that are resistant to prompt injection, unified by a single guiding principle: once an agent has ingested untrusted input, it must be constrained so that input cannot trigger any consequential (integrity- or confidentiality-breaking) action. The six patterns are (1) Action-Selector, (2) Plan-Then-Execute, (3) LLM Map-Reduce, (4) Dual LLM, (5) Code-Then-Execute, and (6) Context-Minimization. The paper's own contribution is the naming, comparative framing (utility-vs-security trade-offs), and the demonstration of applicability across ten case-study application domains (Table 1), rather than the invention of every pattern from scratch. Several patterns are explicitly attributed to prior work by the authors themselves: Dual LLM and the sub-agent/map-reduce idea to Willison (2023b), Plan-Then-Execute to Debenedetti et al. (2024), and Code-Then-Execute to the framework of Debenedetti et al. (2025). The paper's novelty is the consolidation into a composable pattern catalog plus applied case studies, not new empirical algorithms.
threat_model: Prompt injection against LLM agents: an adversary embeds malicious instructions in content the LLM processes so the model deviates from its intended behavior. The paper deliberately does not distinguish direct (end-user-supplied) from indirect (third-party-data) injection, treating both as 'prompt injection.' Attacker goals enumerated include unauthorized tool execution / system-state manipulation, exfiltration of protected data (e.g., via embedded links or markdown images, non-printing-character encoding), manipulation of the agent's reasoning or output, privilege escalation, remote code execution, and denial of service via resource exhaustion. Per-case-study threat models vary: e.g., the OS Assistant attacker controls file contents/filenames to force insecure shell commands or exfiltration; the Email/Calendar attacker is a third party sending crafted emails/invites (the user themself has no incentive to self-inject since the agent runs at user privilege); the Resume-Screening and Product-Recommender attackers hide instructions (small/white text) in resumes or reviews to boost or discredit. The overarching assumption is that the underlying LLM itself is not robust, so security must come from system-level design that constrains the agent regardless of whether the model is fooled.
limitations: (1) No empirical evaluation: 'provable' resistance is argued structurally, not measured, and no attack corpus or agent benchmark is run in the paper. (2) Residual attack surface acknowledged throughout: control-flow-integrity patterns leave action parameters injectable; the quarantined LLM in the Dual LLM pattern is itself still injectable and can emit attacker-controlled output; Map-Reduce still permits malicious files to be mis-categorized within their own scope. (3) Utility cost is significant for the strongest patterns (Action-Selector largely negates the LLM's fuzzy-search value; strict task decomposition is infeasible for some workflows). (4) The patterns constrain agents away from general-purpose autonomy by design, so they do not secure the open-ended general agents most deployments want. (5) Best-practice defenses (sandboxing, user confirmation) are relegated to an appendix and treated as complementary, not sufficient. (6) The paper positions user confirmation and heuristic detectors as brittle (tired verifiers, adversarial-example-susceptible guardrails) without quantifying either.

quantitative_results: No quantitative experiments reported in the extracted text; the paper presents no attack-success rates, benchmark scores, or ablation numbers.
Scope figures stated in text: 6 design patterns are proposed and 10 case-study application domains are analyzed (Table 1 maps patterns to case studies).

Table 1 (qualitative mapping, no numbers): e.g., OS Assistant is marked applicable to Action-Selector, Plan-Then-Execute, Map-Reduce, and Dual LLM; SQL Agent to Code-Then-Execute; Email & Calendar Assistant to Plan/Code-Then-Execute and Dual LLM; Customer Service Chatbot to Action-Selector and Context-Minimization; Product Recommender to Map-Reduce; Resume Screening to Map-Reduce/Dual LLM.
baselines_compared: No experimental baselines. Qualitative comparison categories only: LLM-level defenses (prompt engineering, adversarial training), user-level defenses (human confirmation), and system-level defenses (input/output detectors/filters, isolation mechanisms). Within each case study, naive vulnerable designs (e.g., 'LLM in the shell', 'direct processing of raw resumes/reviews', 'unconstrained agent', topic classifiers, user confirmation) serve as informal comparison points against the proposed patterns.
